threats.title: SQL Injection Authentication Bypass — routes/login.ts:34
threats.scenario: The login handler at routes/login.ts:34 constructs a raw SQL query by directly interpolating req.body.email and security.hash(req.body.password) into a template literal: `SELECT * FROM Users WHERE email = '${req.body.email}' AND password = '...'`. An unauthenticated attacker sends POST /rest/user/login with email set to `' OR 1=1--`, causing the WHERE clause to evaluate true for every row. SQLite returns the first user (id=1, typically the admin), and the application creates a fully-authenticated JWT token with that user's role and identity. The attacker gains admin-level access to the application without knowing any password.
threats.evidence_summary: models.sequelize.query() is called with a template literal that embeds req.body.email without parameterization, at the precise line that handles credential verification.
threats.impact_description: An unauthenticated attacker can log in as any user, including the admin, bypassing all password-based access controls and obtaining a valid JWT token for that account.
threats.controls_in_place: Sequelize ORM is used for non-login queries (parameterized). The login path explicitly bypasses the ORM with raw sequelize.query().
threats.mitigation_title: Replace raw SQL login query with parameterized Sequelize ORM query

steps: Replace the raw `models.sequelize.query()` call in routes/login.ts:34 with `UserModel.findOne({ where: { email: req.body.email, password: security.hash(req.body.password), deletedAt: null } })` using Sequelize's parameterized ORM interface.
Remove the template literal string interpolation entirely — never construct SQL query strings from user-supplied input.
Add an integration test that submits `' OR 1=1--` as the email and verifies a 401 response.

threats.title: UNION SQL Injection via Product Search — routes/search.ts:23
threats.scenario: The search route at routes/search.ts:23 concatenates req.query.q directly into a SELECT statement: `SELECT * FROM Products WHERE ((name LIKE '%${criteria}%' OR description LIKE '%${criteria}%') AND deletedAt IS NULL) ORDER BY name`. An attacker sends GET /rest/products/search?q=')) UNION SELECT sql,2,3,4,5,6,7,8,9 FROM sqlite_master-- to extract the full database schema. A follow-up UNION SELECT id,email,password,role,4,5,6,7,8 FROM Users dumps all user credentials (MD5 hashes) and role assignments. The response is returned as JSON in the normal products array. The search input is truncated to 200 characters but this is sufficient for the UNION payload.
threats.evidence_summary: sequelize.query() is called at line 23 with criteria interpolated from req.query.q into a LIKE clause, with no parameterization or prepared statement.
threats.impact_description: An attacker can exfiltrate all user credentials, email addresses, roles, and the complete database schema from the SQLite database.
threats.controls_in_place: Input length is capped at 200 characters (line 22), which does not prevent SQL injection.
threats.mitigation_title: Parameterize the product search query using Sequelize ORM LIKE operators

steps: Replace the raw sequelize.query() call in routes/search.ts:23 with `ProductModel.findAll({ where: { [Op.or]: [{ name: { [Op.like]: `%${criteria}%` } }, { description: { [Op.like]: `%${criteria}%` } }], deletedAt: null }, order: [['name', 'ASC']] })` using Sequelize's Op.like which generates parameterized LIKE clauses.
Import Op from sequelize: `import { Op } from 'sequelize'`.
Add test coverage verifying that UNION-based payloads return empty results instead of data.

threats.title: Mass Assignment: role Field Writable via POST /api/Users — server.ts:483
threats.scenario: The finale-rest auto-generated REST API registers UserModel with `exclude: ['password', 'totpSecret']` (server.ts:483), but does NOT exclude the `role` field. Finale passes the entire request body to Sequelize's create() method. An unauthenticated attacker sends `POST /api/Users` with body `{"email":"attacker@evil.com","password":"pass","role":"admin"}`. Sequelize's UserModel.create() receives `role: 'admin'` and the model setter at models/user.ts:86-99 accepts it (isIn validator confirms 'admin' is a valid value). The attacker receives a registered account with the admin role, bypassing any privilege gate.
threats.evidence_summary: server.ts:483 shows the finale resource for User excludes only password and totpSecret, leaving role writable; models/user.ts:80-84 confirms role is a string field with defaultValue 'customer' but no write-protection from external input.
threats.impact_description: Any unauthenticated user on the internet can self-register with admin role, gaining full administrative access to the application including access to all user data and administrative operations.
threats.controls_in_place: The role field uses `isIn: [['customer', 'deluxe', 'accounting', 'admin']]` for validation, which only restricts the value to known roles but does not prevent external assignment.
threats.mitigation_title: Add 'role' to excludeAttributes in the finale User resource, and force server-side default

steps: In server.ts:483, change the User resource definition to `{ name: 'User', exclude: ['password', 'totpSecret', 'role', 'deluxeToken', 'totpSecret'], model: UserModel }` so the finale layer strips these fields from the POST body before Sequelize create().

Alternatively, add a `resource.create.fetch.before` hook that explicitly deletes `context.attributes.role` and sets it to the default value 'customer', similar to the wallet creation hook at server.ts:509.
Add a test that POSTs to /api/Users with role='admin' and verifies the created user has role='customer'.

threats.title: NoSQL Injection with multi:true Enables Mass Review Overwrite — routes/updateProductReviews.ts:18-20
threats.scenario: The updateProductReviews handler calls `db.reviewsCollection.update({ _id: req.body.id }, { $set: { message: req.body.message } }, { multi: true })`. When `req.body.id` is supplied as a MarsDB query operator object (e.g. `{ $gt: '' }`) rather than a string ID, the filter matches all documents in the collection. Combined with `multi: true`, this updates every review's message field to the attacker-controlled value, effectively defacing all product reviews simultaneously. No ownership check is enforced in the update filter — the `user` variable (line 16) is read but only used to trigger challenge detection, not to scope the update.
threats.evidence_summary: routes/updateProductReviews.ts:18 uses req.body.id directly as a MarsDB filter with multi:true at line 20, and no UserId/author constraint is added to the filter.
threats.impact_description: An attacker can overwrite the message field of all product reviews in the MarsDB in-memory store, or update a review they did not author (forged review), corrupting review data for all products.
threats.controls_in_place: JWT authentication is required to reach this endpoint (express-jwt middleware applied globally), but ownership of the specific review is not verified.
threats.mitigation_title: Add author ownership constraint to the MarsDB update filter and disable multi:true

steps: Change the update filter to `{ _id: req.body.id, author: user.data.email }` so only the authenticated user's own review can be updated.
Remove `{ multi: true }` from the update options — single-document updates should never use multi.
Validate that req.body.id is a string type before passing it to the MarsDB filter to prevent operator injection.

threats.title: Weak MD5 Password Hashing Without Salt — lib/insecurity.ts:43
threats.scenario: lib/insecurity.ts:43 defines `export const hash = (data: string) => crypto.createHash('md5').update(data).digest('hex')`. This unsalted MD5 hash is applied to every user password before storage (models/user.ts:77). MD5 is not a password hashing function — it is a general-purpose hash with no work factor, no salt, and is trivially reversible for common passwords via rainbow tables or GPU-accelerated brute force. If the SQLite database file (data/juiceshop.sqlite) is exfiltrated — possible via path traversal in file-serving routes or direct container access — all user passwords can be recovered within hours. Under PCI-DSS 8.3.1, password storage must use strong adaptive hashing (bcrypt/scrypt/Argon2). The SQL injection vulnerabilities (data-layer-001 and data-layer-002) make the Users table extractable from a remote attacker without file system access.
threats.evidence_summary: lib/insecurity.ts:43 calls crypto.createHash('md5') for password hashing; models/user.ts:77 calls security.hash(clearTextPassword) in the Sequelize setter, storing the result as the user's password.
threats.impact_description: All user account passwords can be recovered from the database via rainbow tables or brute force, enabling account takeover across the user base.
threats.controls_in_place: None — no salt, no key-stretching, no work factor.
threats.mitigation_title: Replace MD5 password hashing with bcrypt or Argon2 in lib/insecurity.ts

steps: Install bcrypt: `npm install bcrypt @types/bcrypt`.
Replace `crypto.createHash('md5').update(data).digest('hex')` with `bcrypt.hashSync(data, 12)` for hashing and add a `verify` function using `bcrypt.compareSync`.
Update the login query in routes/login.ts to use bcrypt.compare() rather than comparing hash values in the SQL WHERE clause — this is also required to fix data-layer-001.
Migrate existing password hashes: on next login, re-hash the password with bcrypt and update the database record.
Ensure the User model setter in models/user.ts:77 calls the new bcrypt hash function.

threats.title: Unauthenticated Public Exposure of Encryption Key Directory — server.ts:277
threats.scenario: server.ts:277-278 registers `app.use('/encryptionkeys', serveIndex('encryptionkeys', { icons: true, view: 'details' }))` and `app.use('/encryptionkeys/:file', serveKeyFiles())` with no authentication middleware. The directory contains `jwt.pub` (the RSA-2048 public key used to verify JWT tokens) and `premium.key` (used for premium membership entitlement). The RSA private key is hardcoded in lib/insecurity.ts:23 as a multiline string literal. An attacker who retrieves `jwt.pub` can confirm the exact key used for JWT verification, and in combination with the hardcoded private key (also available via source code access), can forge arbitrary JWT tokens for any user identity or role. The `premium.key` exposure allows bypassing premium content gates.
threats.evidence_summary: server.ts:277 mounts serveIndex on /encryptionkeys with no isAuthorized() middleware; routes/keyServer.ts:10-18 confirms files are served from the directory by filename without any auth check.
threats.impact_description: Exposure of jwt.pub combined with the hardcoded private key (lib/insecurity.ts:23) enables JWT forgery, allowing impersonation of any user. Exposure of premium.key enables premium content bypass.
threats.controls_in_place: routes/keyServer.ts:13 rejects filenames containing '/'. No other access control is applied.
threats.mitigation_title: Restrict /encryptionkeys route to authenticated admin users and remove the private key from source code

steps: Add `security.isAuthorized()` and `security.isAdmin()` middleware before the /encryptionkeys route registration in server.ts:277.
Remove the hardcoded RSA private key from lib/insecurity.ts:23 and load it from an environment variable or a secrets manager (e.g. `process.env.JWT_PRIVATE_KEY` or a mounted Kubernetes secret).
Move jwt.pub out of the publicly accessible filesystem or restrict its access to internal services only.
Rotate the RSA key pair after remediation since the private key is exposed in the repository.
remediation.code_example:
// server.ts — add auth before encryptionkeys mount
app.use('/encryptionkeys', security.isAuthorized(), security.isAdmin(),
  serveIndexMiddleware, serveIndex('encryptionkeys', { icons: true, view: 'details' }))
app.use('/encryptionkeys/:file', security.isAuthorized(), security.isAdmin(), serveKeyFiles())

threats.title: No Audit Logging for Data Write Operations — models/index.ts:39
threats.scenario: Sequelize is initialized in models/index.ts:30-40 with `logging: false`, suppressing all SQL query logging. MarsDB (data/mongodb.ts) has no logging configuration. Write operations to sensitive tables — including Users (role changes, account creation), Orders, and product reviews — produce no structured audit record linking the actor, action, affected resource, and timestamp. If an attacker exploits the mass assignment vulnerability (data-layer-003) to elevate their role to admin, or forges a product review via the NoSQL injection (data-layer-004), there is no log entry to detect or reconstruct the event. SOC2 CC7.2 requires that security events be identified and logged.
threats.evidence_summary: models/index.ts:39 sets `logging: false` in the Sequelize constructor options; data/mongodb.ts has no event hooks or logging configured for MarsDB collections.
threats.impact_description: Security-relevant write operations (role changes, account creation, review manipulation) leave no audit trail, making incident detection and forensic reconstruction impossible.
threats.controls_in_place: HTTP access logs are available via /support/logs (server.ts:281), but these are request-level logs without actor-to-action-to-resource resolution.
threats.mitigation_title: Enable structured audit logging for all write operations on sensitive models

steps: Add Sequelize lifecycle hooks (afterCreate, afterUpdate, afterDestroy) on UserModel to emit a structured audit log entry: `{ ts, actor: userId, action: 'create'|'update'|'delete', model: 'User', recordId, changedFields }`.
Write audit logs to an append-only sink (e.g. a separate audit table with no UPDATE/DELETE permissions, or a write-once log stream).
Add MarsDB update/insert hooks in data/mongodb.ts to log review and order write operations.
Change `logging: false` to a structured logger (e.g. `logging: (sql, timing) => auditLogger.info({ sql, timing })`) or keep false but ensure the hooks above cover security-relevant operations.

threats.title: User Role Elevation via Mass Assignment on POST /api/Users — server.ts:483
threats.scenario: This threat expands on data-layer-003 from an EoP perspective. The finale-rest resource for User at server.ts:483 excludes only `['password', 'totpSecret']`. An unauthenticated attacker who registers with `{"role":"admin"}` in the POST body creates an account in the Users SQLite table with role='admin'. On the next login, the JWT issued by the login route (routes/login.ts:23-26) includes the admin role from the database record. All role-based access control downstream (security.isAdmin(), security.isAccounting(), etc.) relies on the role in the JWT, so this single exploit grants unrestricted admin access — including access to /administration panel, user listing at /api/Users (with isAuthorized), and all management functions. The `validate: { isIn: [['customer','deluxe','accounting','admin']] }` at models/user.ts:83-85 confirms all four role values are accepted — the validator is not a security control here.
threats.evidence_summary: server.ts:483 shows `exclude: ['password', 'totpSecret']` without 'role'; models/user.ts:80-84 confirms role is a persisted field accepting 'admin' as a valid input value.
threats.impact_description: An unauthenticated attacker can self-register with admin role, gaining complete privileged access to all application functionality and data.
threats.controls_in_place: No server-side role assignment restriction on user creation.
threats.mitigation_title: Block role assignment in the User creation API by excluding role from finale resource attributes

steps: Update the autoModels User entry in server.ts:483 to `{ name: 'User', exclude: ['password', 'totpSecret', 'role'], model: UserModel }` so finale strips the role field from POST /api/Users request bodies.
Ensure the UserModel Sequelize definition sets `role` to a protected field that can only be modified by server-side logic (not direct client assignment).
Add a server-side validation middleware on POST /api/Users that explicitly deletes req.body.role before passing to finale, as defense-in-depth.
Write an integration test that POSTs with role='admin' and asserts the created user.role equals 'customer'.
